dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3, priority 1 for a sick person at a residence in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the department. What made you call 911, ma'am?
Yes, he was sitting on the bed looking unresponsive. His leg started throbbing, and he feels weak.
Can you tell us what's wrong, sir? Open your eyes.
Uhh, yes... Hmm, my leg feels weak.
Can you tell me your name and where we are, sir?
He is responding to verbal commands. He seems to have weakness on the right side.
Airway is patent and clear. No obstructions or sounds of an issue.
Breathing is spontaneous and fast, about 38 a minute. Lungs clear bilaterally.
Pulse is strong at 63. Blood pressure is 132/86. Skin is pink, warm, and dry. No bleeding or abnormal circulation.
When did the pain in your right leg start, sir? Throbbing or cramping?
It started maybe ten minutes ago... Throbbing.
Do you have any allergies, sir?
Any medications you're taking now, sir?
He had a brain tumor removed five months ago at [COMPANY_NAME]. Now has skin cancer too.
Can you tell me your age, sir?
I'm 62. Name is Harold.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate your pain now, sir?
Maybe 6 overall. It started suddenly around 11:45.
Does the pain get worse with movement or anything else?
It throbs and it cramps. No pulsating sensation, though.
Is it constant or did it start and stop in between?
Constant these last ten minutes, but I've had little cramps longer than that.
Skin looks good-pink, warm, dry. No rashes or signs of injury.
Your eyes are reacting well. Pupils are normal size and round.
Chest is symmetrical and not tender. No signs of injury.
His right leg appears to have some noticeable contractures.
Pulse is 63 and regular. Strong peripheral pulses.
Respirations is 38, unlabored. Normal breath sounds bilaterally.
Blood pressure is 132/86.
SpO₂ is 95. Let's see... he doesn't need extra flow right now.
Blood glucose is showing 99. No hypoglycemia here.
Let's hook you up for a 12-lead EKG. Partner, we'll need to do a 4-lead monitoring first.
4-lead monitoring established successfully.
Alright, 12-lead EKG is done and shows sinus tachycardia.
IV started in the left arm using an 18-gauge. Clot-free and running smoothly.
Based on findings, we are activating the stroke protocol (Protocol 3-5). Transporting to [COMPANY_NAME] for CT.
His pulse is now 159. Looks like an increase in heart rate.
Respirations are at 35 and BP is 117/68. SpO₂ is up to 98.
Tremors are more severe now. Contractures are more pronounced.
Pain remains at 6. Any change since the first time?
He says the pain is now more of a stiffness on his right side.
Any changes in speech, vision, or alertness?
No, just weakness. Can't feel my fingers on the right.
Harold, would you like to be taken to [COMPANY_NAME] or another hospital?
[COMPANY_NAME]. They've treated me before.
We'll move you now. Two of us will lift you to the stretcher safely.
He can walk a little, but be gentle with him after the surgery.
Got him. He's ambulatory but appears to be in minor situational distress.
Stretcher loaded and secured. Ready for transport.
Placing an ETCO₂ nasal cannula for better breathing assessment.
GCS is at 15. Alert and oriented to all four parts.
EKG has been continuously monitored from the 4-lead setup and now upgraded to 12-lead.
Now shows sinus tachycardia on the 12-lead. No prior readings to compare.
Let's document the new-onset right extremity contractures, which might indicate a neurological issue.
We are following the medical-stroke/tia protocol for transport.
Patient will be handed off at [COMPANY_NAME] for CT scan. RN will assume care.
Patient was moved using a two-person carry and was secured on the stretcher.
All transport stages completed without incident.
Harold reported feeling more stable once secured for transport.
Vital signs remain stable on transport. No signs of deterioration.
Signs point to possible neurological compromise. Stroke protocol remains in effect.
Per his request, transported directly to [COMPANY_NAME] for familiarity with care team.
Patient remains stable on transport vitals with no alarms or deterioration.
Continuous EKG monitoring is intact and functional during transport.
The GCS of 15 remains consistent throughout the transport.
No reported dizziness or vision issues at this time.
Trunk weakness and right-sided issues are persistent and reported by both him and the wife.
No current pain report, and patient denies any new-onset discomfort.
Using a sheet pull method for safe transfer from stretcher to hospital bed.
No signs of injury on the neurological exam, but persistent tremors on the right side.
His wife reports the brain tumor was partially removed five months ago-relevant for neurological assessment.
Patient is conscious and able to engage, with baseline ambulation compromised now.
All readings stabilize during last minute of transport.
IV continues flowing without issues. He is arranged for comfort.
SpO₂ stable at 98 with no concern for oxygen adjacency.
Patient remains calm and talkative during transport.
At [COMPANY_NAME], patient will be taken to CT Room 1. RN will assume primary care.
Patient is now lethargic but still conscious and alert on current observation.
Let's note the patient was initially found somewhat lethargic. No further declines since.
Medical team is aware of medical history and current findings.
Hospital team is prepared with pre-arrival documentation and rapid CT evaluation.
Stroke suspicion was the primary driver in EMS protocol choice.
Rhythmic changes such as tachycardia are documented on EKG.
The transport was handled with proper protocols continuously on activation.
Stroke protocol was followed to align with field assessment and vital signs.
Patient felt stable throughout and no additional problems arose.
Hospital team was ready with pre-arrival documentation as instructed.
Protocol continued through full transport without protocol revision or deviation.
Safe arrival was confirmed by both EMS and hospital staff.
The complete stroke/TIA protocol was used to guide this transport.
Full report is provided to the hospital team for continuity of care.
Final impression remains neurological and consistent with stroke concern.
EMS report is complete, and patient care is safely transferred to the hospital team.
EKG details remain consistent with prior readings and were flagged for hospital staff.
Patient is transported timely to [COMPANY_NAME] with all relevant information documented.
